question: [File: 'File Name: rest/tokens.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: extractSqlFromTokenBalancesRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the 'distinct on (ti.account_id)' clause in the timestamp-filtered balance query to ensure only the latest balance per account is returned within the snapshot window, and by choosing a timestamp just before a transfer, make it appear that the transfer never occurred in the balance record?

[File: 'File Name: rest/tokens.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTokenBalances()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a timestamp filter to getTokenBalances() combined with a balance filter to identify accounts whose balance changed between two time points, effectively reconstructing which accounts were involved in a specific transfer transaction without having direct access to transaction records?

[File: 'File Name: rest/tokens.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: formatTokenBalanceRow()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that formatTokenBalanceRow() returns the decimals value from the TokenService cache (which may be null if the token is not cached) to infer whether a token has been recently queried, leaking cache state information that reveals which tokens are actively being monitored?

[File: 'File Name: rest/tokens.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTokenBalances()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a very large limit parameter to getTokenBalances() to retrieve all token holders in a single response, causing the database to perform a full scan of the token_account table for a popular token, resulting in a denial-of-service condition that prevents legitimate balance queries from completing?

[File: 'File Name: rest/tokens.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: extractSqlFromTokenBalancesRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a timestamp filter to extractSqlFromTokenBalancesRequest() where balances.getAccountBalanceTimestampRange() returns undefined for the lower bound, causing the function to return an empty object and getTokenBalances() to silently return an empty response, making it appear that no accounts hold the token at that timestamp?

[File: 'File Name: rest/tokens.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTokenInfoRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a timestamp filter to getTokenInfoRequest() that resolves to a time when the token's freeze_key or kyc_key was different, causing the API to return historical key data that misrepresents the current authorization requirements for token transactions, leading integrators to submit transactions with incorrect key assumptions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/tokens.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: formatTokenInfoRow()] Can an unprivileged external user query getTokenInfoRequest() and observe the pause_status field to determine whether a token is currently paused, then use this information to time transaction submissions to avoid paused states, effectively using the mirror node as a real-time oracle for token operational state?

[File: 'File Name: rest/tokens.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: formatTokenInfoRow()] Can an unprivileged external user query getTokenInfoRequest() and observe the freeze_default field to determine whether new token associations are frozen by default, using this to predict whether a newly associated account can immediately participate in token transfers without a freeze transaction?

[File: 'File Name: rest/tokens.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTokenInfoRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a timestamp filter to getTokenInfoRequest() that resolves to a time when the token's supply_key was set to a non-null value, while the current state has supply_key as null, causing the API to return historical key data that misrepresents the current minting/burning authorization, leading to incorrect transaction construction?

[File: 'File Name: rest/tokens.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: formatTokenInfoRow()] Can an unprivileged external user query getTokenInfoRequest() and observe the total_supply and max_supply fields to calculate the remaining mintable supply of a token, using this information to front-run mint transactions or predict when a token will reach its maximum supply?

[File: 'File Name: rest/tokens.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTokenInfoRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a timestamp filter to getTokenInfoRequest() that resolves to a time just before a token's total_supply changed (due to a mint or burn transaction), causing the API to return a stale total_supply that misrepresents the current circulating supply and misleads downstream financial calculations?
